status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Enterprise knowledge management with wiki, FAQ, decision tree, AI-powered search, content freshness scoring, and expertise directory.

plan.description: Gather and document business requirements for the enterprise knowledge management platform. Define user personas (knowledge contributors, consumers, admins), core workflows (create/review/approve/retire content), ROI model (support ticket deflection, onboarding speed), and process flows for wiki, FAQ, decision tree, and expertise directory modules.
acceptance_criteria: Business requirements document covers all 6 modules with user stories in Given/When/Then format
At least 4 user personas defined with goals, pain points, and usage frequency
ROI model includes baseline metrics and projected improvements with data sources
Process flows cover create, review, publish, update, retire lifecycle for each content type
Stakeholder sign-off obtained on requirements document
Integration points with existing systems mapped
Migration plan drafted

plan.description: Create the product requirements document (PRD), prioritized roadmap, and success metrics framework. Define MVP scope vs. v2 features. Establish KPIs: search relevance score, content freshness index, time-to-answer, expert utilization rate, and knowledge reuse rate.
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all functional and non-functional requirements with priority (P0/P1/P2)
MVP scope clearly separated from future phases with rationale
Each success metric has a baseline, target, and measurement method
Roadmap spans 3 phases with milestones and dependencies
PRD reviewed and approved by product stakeholders

plan.description: Draft terms of service, privacy policy, and data processing agreements. Review IP ownership for user-generated content in the wiki. Define content licensing model. Assess GDPR/CCPA implications for the expertise directory (personal data of employees). Review OSS license compatibility for planned dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: Privacy policy explicitly covers expertise directory personal data collection and retention
Terms of service include content ownership, acceptable use, and DMCA takedown provisions
DPA template covers all sub-processors with SCCs for EU data transfer
OSS license audit lists all planned dependencies with license type and compatibility risk
Legal review sign-off documented

plan.description: Produce ISO 27001 and SOC 2 readiness artifacts. Define the information security management scope statement, asset inventory, and risk treatment plan for the knowledge management system. Map controls to SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria (Security, Availability, Confidentiality). Create initial compliance evidence tracker.
acceptance_criteria: ISMS scope statement defines boundaries, assets, and exclusions
Asset inventory covers all data stores, services, and third-party integrations
Risk treatment plan assigns owner and treatment to each identified risk
SOC 2 control mapping covers all applicable CC criteria with control descriptions
Evidence tracker lists each control with collection method and frequency

plan.description: Conduct user research and produce wireframes for all major flows: wiki article create/edit, FAQ builder, decision tree editor (visual node graph), AI search interface, content freshness dashboard, and expertise directory profile pages. Define information architecture, navigation hierarchy, and accessibility requirements (WCAG 2.1 AA).
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes cover all 8 identified pages at medium-fidelity
Information architecture diagram shows all navigation paths and content hierarchy
Decision tree editor wireframe supports visual drag-and-drop node creation
Accessibility checklist maps each UI component to WCAG 2.1 AA criteria
Design system tokens defined for color, typography, spacing, and elevation
Usability test with 5 users conducted and findings documented

plan.description: Design the database schema for all knowledge management entities: articles (wiki), FAQ entries, decision tree nodes and edges, expertise profiles, tags, categories, search indices, freshness scores, and audit logs. Define indexing strategy for full-text search. Plan vector embedding storage for AI search. Write migrations.
acceptance_criteria: ERD covers all entities with cardinality and foreign key constraints
Schema includes soft-delete, created_at/updated_at on all mutable entities
pgvector table defined with 1536-dim embedding column and HNSW index
ContentVersion table supports full version history with diff metadata
Migrations are idempotent and reversible (up/down)
All PII fields in ExpertProfile documented in data dictionary

plan.description: Design the OpenAPI 3.0 specification for all REST endpoints across modules: wiki CRUD, FAQ CRUD, decision tree CRUD, search (keyword + semantic), freshness score API, expertise directory CRUD, user management, permissions, webhooks, and admin endpoints. Define authentication (JWT + API key), rate limiting headers, and pagination contracts.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates without errors against OpenAPI 3.0 schema
Every endpoint has request/response schemas, status codes, and examples
Authentication schemes documented with token acquisition flow
Rate limiting headers (X-RateLimit-*) specified on all endpoints
Webhook payload schemas defined for content published/updated/retired events
Search endpoint supports both keyword and semantic (vector) query modes

plan.description: Produce a threat model (STRIDE) for the knowledge management system. Identify attack surfaces: authentication, content injection, search injection (prompt injection for AI search), access control bypass, and data exfiltration via expertise directory. Define security controls and produce a penetration test plan.
acceptance_criteria: Threat model covers all 6 attack surfaces with STRIDE categories
Each threat has likelihood, impact, and risk rating
Security controls map to ISO 27001 Annex A and SOC 2 CC6 criteria
Prompt injection mitigations specified for AI search input sanitization
SBOM generated listing all direct and transitive dependencies with CVE scan
Penetration test plan covers scope, test cases, and acceptance criteria

plan.description: Set up the agentic AI pattern for the platform's AI-powered features. Configure a Multi-Agent Coordinator pattern: a coordinator agent routes queries to specialist agents (wiki search agent, FAQ match agent, decision tree traversal agent, expertise recommender agent). Set up embedding pipeline config, LLM gateway config, and vector store connection. Define system prompts for each specialist agent.
acceptance_criteria: agent_config.yaml defines all 5 agents with roles, tools, and routing rules
system_prompts.yaml contains tested prompts for each specialist agent
Coordinator correctly routes a test query to the appropriate specialist agent
Embedding pipeline config specifies model, batch size, and retry policy
LLM provider is configurable via environment variable with no code changes
Prompt injection guard rules defined in coordinator system prompt

plan.description: Build the core backend services: authentication/authorization (JWT, RBAC with roles: viewer, contributor, editor, admin), user management, and shared middleware (rate limiting, request logging, audit trail, tenant isolation). Implement the project scaffolding: FastAPI application factory, dependency injection, database session management, and health check endpoints.
acceptance_criteria: JWT tokens issued with configurable expiry and refresh token rotation
RBAC middleware enforces role permissions on all protected endpoints
Audit log records all write operations with user_id, timestamp, entity, and action
Rate limiting returns 429 with Retry-After header when exceeded
Health check endpoint returns database, cache, and search engine status
All middleware covered by unit tests with >85% coverage

plan.description: Implement the Wiki module backend: article CRUD with rich text (Markdown/ProseMirror JSON), version history, draft/published/archived states, category and tag management, internal linking, media attachment handling, and collaborative editing support via operational transforms or CRDT-based conflict resolution.
acceptance_criteria: Article CRUD endpoints match OpenAPI spec with correct status codes
Version history stores full diffs and allows point-in-time restore
State machine enforces valid transitions (draft→review→published→archived)
Internal link graph maintained and broken links detected on article delete
Media attachments validated for type and size, stored in object storage
Concurrent edits handled without data loss (last-write-wins or CRDT)

plan.description: Implement the FAQ module backend: question-answer pair CRUD, category grouping, related FAQ suggestions, upvote/downvote feedback, auto-archival of stale FAQs, and bulk import/export (CSV/JSON). Include a FAQ clustering engine that groups semantically similar questions.
acceptance_criteria: FAQ CRUD endpoints match OpenAPI spec
Related FAQ suggestions return top-5 semantically similar entries using embeddings
Upvote/downvote updates helpfulness score and triggers re-ranking
Auto-archival rule fires after configurable inactivity period (default: 180 days)
Bulk import accepts CSV with header validation and row-level error reporting
Semantic clustering groups FAQs into topic clusters on demand

plan.description: Implement the Decision Tree module backend: node and edge CRUD, tree validation (no cycles, no orphaned nodes), traversal engine (given answers, returns next node or terminal result), version snapshots, conditional branching logic, and decision tree export to JSON/Mermaid diagram format.
acceptance_criteria: Tree validation rejects cycles and detects orphaned nodes on save
Traversal engine processes a complete path from root to leaf given test inputs
Conditional branches support operators: equals, contains, greater_than, less_than, regex
Version snapshot captures full tree state and allows rollback
Export to Mermaid produces valid flowchart syntax renderable in browser
Traversal engine handles missing required answers with a clear error code

plan.description: Implement the Expertise Directory module backend: employee profile CRUD (skills, certifications, domain tags, availability), endorsement system, skill taxonomy management, org hierarchy integration (LDAP/SCIM sync), profile completeness scoring, and expert recommendation engine (given a topic, returns ranked experts).
acceptance_criteria: Profile CRUD enforces consent flags before exposing PII fields
Endorsement prevents self-endorsement and duplicate endorsements per endorser
LDAP/SCIM sync processes delta updates without duplicating records
Profile completeness score computed from weighted field completion (0–100)
Expert recommendation returns ranked list with skill match score for a given topic
Skill taxonomy supports hierarchical tags with parent-child relationships

plan.description: Build the AI-powered search pipeline: document ingestion and chunking, embedding generation (text-embedding-3-small), vector storage in pgvector, hybrid search (BM25 keyword + cosine similarity vector), re-ranking with a cross-encoder model, and the Multi-Agent Coordinator orchestration layer that routes queries to the appropriate specialist agent based on query intent classification.
acceptance_criteria: Ingestion pipeline processes wiki, FAQ, and decision tree content into chunks ≤512 tokens
Embedding generation batches documents with retry on rate limit errors
Hybrid search combines BM25 and vector scores with configurable alpha weight
Re-ranker improves top-10 retrieval MRR by ≥15% on test query set
Coordinator correctly classifies query intent and routes to correct specialist agent in ≥90% of test cases
Search response includes source document, chunk, relevance score, and content type
End-to-end search latency P95 ≤ 2 seconds under 50 concurrent users

plan.description: Implement the Content Freshness Scoring module: rule-based and ML-based staleness detection, configurable decay functions per content type, automated freshness score computation (0–100), scheduled re-evaluation jobs, owner notification system for stale content, and freshness score API for dashboard and search ranking integration.
acceptance_criteria: Freshness score computed from ≥4 configurable signals with weight configuration
Decay function applied per content type (wiki: 90-day linear, FAQ: 60-day exponential)
Scheduled job runs daily and updates scores for all published content
Owner notification sent when score drops below configurable threshold (default: 40)
Freshness score exposed on search results and used as ranking signal boost
Score history logged for trend analysis with daily granularity

plan.description: Implement the HITL (Human-in-the-Loop) agentic pattern for AI-generated content suggestions and expert recommendations. When the AI search agent proposes an answer synthesis or the expertise recommender suggests a new expert profile tag, the system creates a proposal in the approval queue. Editors/admins review and approve before the change is published. Use iterative_refinement for auto-improvement of generated content drafts.
acceptance_criteria: Every AI-generated content modification creates a proposal record before any DB write
Proposal store tracks status: pending, approved, rejected, expired
Approved proposals execute the associated action and log the approval chain
Rejected proposals feed correction back to the refinement agent for learning
Iterative refinement loops a maximum of 3 times before escalating to human
Proposal expiry auto-rejects after configurable TTL (default: 7 days)

plan.description: Build the React frontend scaffold: project setup (Vite + TypeScript + React 18), routing (React Router v6), state management (Zustand), API client layer (typed fetch with OpenAPI-generated types), authentication context (JWT storage, refresh, logout), global error boundary, and loading/skeleton components. Implement the design system from UX tokens.
acceptance_criteria: TypeScript compiles with strict mode and zero errors
API client generated from OpenAPI spec with all endpoints typed
Auth context persists JWT across page reloads and handles token expiry with refresh
Global error boundary catches unhandled errors and shows user-friendly fallback
Design system components (Button, Input, Badge, Card, Modal) match UX wireframes
Lighthouse accessibility score ≥ 90 on the component library page

plan.description: Build the Wiki editor UI: rich text editor (TipTap with ProseMirror), article tree sidebar with drag-and-drop hierarchy, version history timeline with diff viewer, collaborative editing indicator, internal link autocomplete, tag/category picker, media upload with drag-and-drop, and publish/review workflow status badge.
acceptance_criteria: Rich text editor supports headings, lists, code blocks, tables, images, and inline links
Article hierarchy sidebar supports drag-and-drop reordering with parent assignment
Version history timeline shows all versions with author, timestamp, and change summary
Diff viewer highlights added/removed content inline with color coding
Internal link autocomplete suggests articles as user types [[
Media upload accepts images/PDFs up to 25MB with progress indicator
Workflow status badge reflects current state and shows available transitions

plan.description: Build the FAQ builder UI, Decision Tree visual editor, and Expertise Directory UI. FAQ builder: question/answer form with category picker, related FAQ panel, and voting controls. Decision tree editor: visual node graph (ReactFlow) with drag-and-drop node creation, edge connection, condition builder, and traversal simulator. Expertise directory: searchable profile cards, skill filter, org chart view, and endorsement flow.
acceptance_criteria: Decision tree editor renders nodes and edges with ReactFlow and allows drag-and-drop placement
Condition builder supports all operators defined in backend with form validation
Traversal simulator steps through tree path interactively given user inputs
Expertise profile cards display skill tags, endorsement count, and availability
Skill filter supports multi-select with hierarchical taxonomy display
Org chart view renders reporting hierarchy from LDAP/SCIM data
Endorsement flow prevents duplicate endorsements with real-time feedback

plan.description: Build the AI Search UI: conversational search interface with streaming response display, source citation cards with relevance scores, search mode toggle (keyword/semantic/hybrid), content type filters, search history, saved searches, and feedback mechanism (thumbs up/down per result). Integrate the freshness score indicator on all content display surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Streaming response renders tokens progressively without layout shift
Source citation cards show document title, content type, excerpt, and freshness score
Search mode toggle switches between keyword/semantic/hybrid without page reload
Content type filters (wiki/faq/decision_tree) update results in real time
Search history persists across sessions in localStorage with 50-entry limit
Thumbs feedback submits to backend and shows confirmation without interrupting UX
Freshness badge renders green/amber/red based on configurable score thresholds

plan.description: Build the Admin Panel UI: user management table (invite, role assignment, deactivate), module configuration (enable/disable features per role), content freshness dashboard (staleness heatmap, owner notification controls), HITL proposal review queue, audit log viewer with filters, and system health dashboard.
acceptance_criteria: User management table supports invite by email with role pre-assignment
Role assignment changes take effect immediately on next user request
Freshness dashboard heatmap renders a matrix of content age vs. view frequency
Proposal review queue displays pending AI proposals with approve/reject actions
Audit log viewer filters by user, action, entity type, and date range
Health dashboard shows real-time status of database, vector store, search engine, and LLM gateway

plan.description: Build the data pipeline for search index synchronization: event-driven ingestion (Postgres CDC or webhook triggers), incremental embedding updates, Elasticsearch index management (aliases, zero-downtime reindex), and analytics data warehouse (user search patterns, content usage, expert utilization). Implement the freshness signal aggregation pipeline.
acceptance_criteria: CDC pipeline propagates content changes to search index within 30 seconds
Zero-downtime reindex uses index aliases with blue/green swap
Freshness aggregator runs on schedule and writes scores to freshness_scores table
Analytics ETL captures search query, result clicked, dwell time, and feedback per session
Pipeline failures are logged with retry policy and alerting on repeated failure
Incremental embedding updates only re-embed changed chunks to minimize cost

plan.description: Set up Docker Compose for local development (PostgreSQL, pgvector, Redis, Elasticsearch, backend, frontend) and Kubernetes manifests for production deployment. Define resource limits, liveness/readiness probes, horizontal pod autoscaler, persistent volume claims for vector store, and network policies for internal service communication.
acceptance_criteria: docker-compose up starts all services with a single command in < 3 minutes
All K8s manifests pass kubectl --dry-run=client validation
HPA configured for backend deployment with CPU threshold 70% and min/max replicas 2/10
Network policies restrict inter-service communication to documented paths only
PVC for pgvector and Elasticsearch configured with retain reclaim policy
Liveness probes for all services with appropriate initialDelaySeconds

plan.description: Build the CI/CD pipeline: GitHub Actions workflows for PR checks (lint, type-check, unit tests, security scan), staging deployment on merge to main, and production deployment with manual approval gate. Set up monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana), structured logging (Loki), distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry → Jaeger), and alerting rules for SLA breaches.
acceptance_criteria: PR check workflow runs lint, mypy, pytest, and Trivy scan in parallel under 10 minutes
Staging deploy runs automatically on merge to main with smoke tests
Production deploy requires manual approval and runs canary at 10% traffic for 5 minutes
Prometheus scrapes metrics from all backend pods with 15s interval
Grafana dashboard shows: request rate, latency P50/P95/P99, error rate, search latency, freshness job status
Alert fires within 2 minutes of threshold breach in staging and production

plan.description: Write comprehensive unit and integration tests for all backend modules: auth/RBAC, wiki CRUD, FAQ CRUD, decision tree CRUD and traversal, expertise directory, freshness scoring, AI search pipeline, and HITL proposal system. Target ≥85% coverage. Use pytest with fixtures and factory_boy for test data generation.
acceptance_criteria: pytest runs all unit tests in < 5 minutes with no flakiness
Integration tests use real PostgreSQL (testcontainers) not mocks
Coverage report shows ≥85% line coverage across all src/ modules
Decision tree cycle detection tested with graph fixtures containing cycles
HITL proposal tests verify no DB write occurs before proposal approval
Search pipeline tests validate BM25 and vector retrieval independently
Auth tests cover all RBAC role combinations for each endpoint

plan.description: Produce the QA test plan, test case library, and traceability matrix mapping requirements to test cases. Define test environments, entry/exit criteria, defect severity levels, and regression suite scope. Execute exploratory testing sessions on AI search quality, freshness scoring accuracy, and decision tree traversal edge cases.
acceptance_criteria: Test plan covers all PRD requirements with coverage percentage
Traceability matrix maps every P0/P1 requirement to at least one test case
Exploratory testing sessions documented with session charters and findings
AI search quality evaluation uses NDCG metric on 50-query golden set
Freshness scoring accuracy validated against manually labeled staleness dataset of 100 items
Regression suite automated via Playwright covering all critical user journeys

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system integration tests across the full stack: complete knowledge lifecycle (create wiki article → index → search → retrieve → freshen), expertise recommendation flow, decision tree publish-and-traverse flow, and HITL approval pipeline. Run load tests (k6) simulating 200 concurrent users and measure SLA compliance.
acceptance_criteria: Full knowledge lifecycle E2E test passes: create → index → search returns the created article
HITL E2E test verifies no content change persists without an approved proposal record
Decision tree E2E test traverses a 10-node tree to correct terminal node
Load test at 200 users shows search P95 ≤ 2000ms and error rate ≤ 0.1%
All system test scenarios pass in staging environment before production deploy
System test report documents pass/fail status for all test scenarios
Load test simulates expected concurrent users
Failover scenario tested

plan.description: Collect and organize ISO 27001 and SOC 2 evidence artifacts: access control logs, encryption configuration screenshots, vulnerability scan reports (Trivy + OWASP ZAP), penetration test results, incident response plan, change management records, and vendor risk assessments. Produce the SOC 2 readiness report and ISO 27001 Statement of Applicability.
acceptance_criteria: SOC 2 readiness report covers all 5 Trust Service Criteria with control maturity ratings
ISO 27001 Statement of Applicability lists all Annex A controls with justification for exclusions
Trivy scan shows zero critical CVEs in production container images
OWASP ZAP scan report shows no high-severity findings unmitigated
Incident response plan tested with tabletop exercise and results documented
All evidence artifacts timestamped, version-controlled, and linked to control IDs

plan.description: Write operational runbooks: deployment runbook, rollback procedure, database backup/restore, Elasticsearch index recovery, LLM gateway failover, freshness job manual trigger, on-call escalation matrix. Define SLAs (99.5% availability, P95 search < 2s). Create incident severity classification and response time targets.
acceptance_criteria: Deployment runbook tested end-to-end by someone other than the author
Rollback runbook restores previous version within 15 minutes
Database backup/restore runbook validated with a restore drill in staging
SLA document defines availability, latency, and recovery time objectives
Incident severity levels (P0–P3) defined with response time and escalation path
Capacity plan projects resource needs for 3x current load with scaling trigger points

plan.description: Produce user-facing documentation and training materials: end-user guide (wiki authoring, FAQ creation, decision tree building, search tips), admin guide (user management, module config, freshness tuning, compliance reports), API developer guide, and onboarding checklist for new team members. Create video script outlines for 3 key workflows.
acceptance_criteria: User guide covers all 6 modules with step-by-step screenshots
Admin guide includes freshness threshold configuration with before/after examples
API developer guide includes code examples in Python and JavaScript for auth and search
Onboarding checklist validated by a new user completing it without assistance
Video scripts written for: wiki article creation, AI search, and decision tree traversal
All docs reviewed for accuracy against final implemented features
Admin guide separate from end-user guide
Role-based training paths defined

flaws: Step 11 lists 'collaborative editing support via operational transforms or CRDT-based conflict resolution' as one bullet among many wiki features. Real-time collaborative editing (à la Google Docs) is a multi-month engineering effort requiring a WebSocket server, presence protocol, and conflict resolution engine. No WebSocket infrastructure is mentioned anywhere in the plan. This will either be silently dropped or block the entire wiki module.

Step 15 acceptance criterion: 'Re-ranker improves top-10 retrieval MRR by ≥15% on test query set.' There is no golden query set established in any earlier step, and no baseline MRR measured. This criterion is unverifiable as written — you cannot guarantee a relative improvement without a measured baseline.

Step 18 acceptance criterion mentions JWT storage across page reloads. The plan never specifies where JWT tokens are stored. If implemented in localStorage (the default for most React JWT tutorials), this is a P0 XSS vulnerability — any injected script exfiltrates the token. HttpOnly cookies are required for enterprise auth.

Step 23 requires Postgres logical replication (CDC) but wal_level=logical must be set at Postgres initialization time. This is a database configuration concern that belongs in Step 6 (database schema) or Step 24 (infrastructure), not Step 23. If the DB is deployed without it, enabling CDC requires a Postgres restart — production downtime.

Elasticsearch 8.x uses SSPL licensing for production self-hosted deployments in many configurations. Enterprise production use typically requires a commercial Elastic license. The plan has no licensing cost analysis or mitigation (e.g., OpenSearch as a drop-in alternative). This is a legal and budget risk that could force a mid-project replatform.

Step 16 lists 'semantic_drift' as a freshness scoring signal with no specification of how it is computed. Detecting semantic drift requires periodically re-embedding content and comparing cosine distance to a baseline — this is a non-trivial ML pipeline not described anywhere. It will either be silently omitted or require significant unplanned work.

Step 14 (LDAP/SCIM sync) has no conflict resolution strategy defined. In large enterprises, SCIM delta syncs frequently produce duplicate identity records when external IDs change (e.g., contractor rehire, email change). No de-duplication logic or sync idempotency guarantee is specified.

Step 28 lists 'Failover scenario tested' as an acceptance criterion with zero specification of what failover means (DB primary failure? LLM provider outage? Elasticsearch node loss?), what the expected behavior is, or what the RTO target is. This criterion cannot be evaluated.

Steps 19–21 build the frontend but Step 28 (system tests) only depends on Step 22 (admin panel). If any of steps 19–21 are delayed or broken, the E2E tests in Step 28 still pass its dependency check — the dependency graph does not enforce that all frontend modules are complete before system testing.
suggestions: Split Step 11 into two steps: (a) wiki CRUD + versioning + states, and (b) collaborative editing as a separate, explicitly-scoped step with WebSocket infrastructure (socket.io or native WebSocket), a defined CRDTlibrary (e.g., Yjs or Automerge), and its own acceptance criteria. If timeline is constrained, make collaborative editing a v2 feature with a clear deferral decision in the PRD.

Step 6 must specify wal_level=logical in the Postgres initialization config and reserve a replication slot for the CDC pipeline. Document this in the schema migration README so any fresh DB provisioning includes it.

Replace the relative MRR acceptance criterion in Step 15 with an absolute target or define a mandatory baseline measurement task before Step 15 begins. For example: 'BM25-only baseline MRR on 50-query golden set measured and recorded in eval/baseline.json before re-ranker integration.'
Step 18 must explicitly mandate httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies for JWT storage. Add this as a P0 acceptance criterion. Remove any guidance that implies localStorage for tokens.

Add SSO/SAML 2.0 (or OIDC) as an explicit module. Enterprise knowledge management without SSO will fail procurement reviews. This belongs in Step 7 (API design) and Step 10 (auth implementation) at minimum.

Replace Elasticsearch with OpenSearch (Apache 2.0 license) or include an Elastic commercial license line item in the project budget and legal review (Step 3). The current plan has an unacknowledged licensing risk.

Add a data export / portability endpoint (wiki articles as ZIP/Markdown, FAQ as CSV, decision trees as JSON) to the API design in Step 7. Enterprise buyers require this for vendor lock-in compliance — its absence is a common deal-breaker.

Step 15 cross-encoder re-ranking at P95 ≤ 2s under 50 concurrent users requires either a GPU-backed inference service or aggressive result caching. Add an explicit architecture decision: either deploy the cross-encoder behind a dedicated inference endpoint (TorchServe/Triton) or cache re-ranked results by query hash with a short TTL.
Add Steps 19–21 as explicit dependencies of Step 28 to enforce that all frontend modules are complete before system testing runs.
missing: SSO/SAML 2.0 or OIDC integration — not mentioned anywhere. Mandatory for enterprise customer procurement.
WebSocket server design — required by Step 11 (collaborative editing) and Step 21 (streaming search responses). No WebSocket infrastructure, protocol, or connection management is defined.
Content sanitization for the rich text wiki editor — ProseMirror/TipTap output can contain arbitrary HTML if misconfigured. No mention of DOMPurify or server-side HTML sanitization before storage.

LLM API cost model — the plan uses text-embedding-3-small for ingestion, similarity clustering, freshness drift, expert recommendation, and search. At enterprise scale (thousands of documents, frequent re-indexing), this is a significant recurring cost with no budget estimate or cost cap.

Multi-tenancy model — 'enterprise' typically means multiple departments or customer organizations. The plan defines RBAC roles but never defines whether this is a single-tenant or multi-tenant SaaS deployment. If multi-tenant, every data model and query must be scoped by tenant_id from day one.

Email/notification infrastructure — Step 16 sends freshness alerts to content owners; Step 17 routes HITL proposals. No email provider (SES, SendGrid), template system, or notification preferences model is defined.

File storage service — Step 11 references storing media attachments in 'object storage' but no S3/GCS bucket configuration, CDN setup, signed URL generation, or storage lifecycle policy is specified anywhere.
Malware scanning for file uploads — Step 11 validates type and size but does not scan uploaded files for malware. This is a P0 security control for any enterprise platform accepting user uploads.

pgvector backup and recovery strategy — if the embedding store is corrupted or the PVC is lost, re-embedding all content from scratch can take hours and cost hundreds of dollars. No backup schedule or point-in-time recovery for pgvector is defined.

Data migration tooling — Step 1 mentions a 'migration plan drafted' as a deliverable but no migration step exists in the build plan. Enterprises adopting this platform will have existing knowledge bases (Confluence, SharePoint, Notion) that need import tooling.
API versioning strategy — the OpenAPI spec in Step 7 has no versioning scheme (/v1/, header-based, etc.). Breaking changes in a future version will break all integrations.
security_risks: JWT token storage location unspecified — if defaulted to localStorage, all active sessions are exfiltrable via any XSS vector in the rich text wiki editor or search results. Severity: Critical.

ProseMirror/TipTap rich text output stored and re-rendered without explicit server-side sanitization — a malicious contributor can inject stored XSS payloads that execute for all viewers of a wiki article. Severity: Critical.

Prompt injection in AI search — Step 8 identifies this risk and Step 9 says 'Prompt injection guard rules defined in coordinator system prompt.' System prompt instructions are not a reliable defense; they are bypassable. No input-layer sanitization, output validation, or sandboxed LLM execution model is specified. Severity: High.

File upload endpoint (Step 11) validates type and size but has no malware scanning. A contributor can upload a crafted PDF or SVG containing embedded JavaScript or exploit code. In an enterprise platform, this file is then served to potentially thousands of users. Severity: High.

LDAP/SCIM integration (Step 14) — no mention of LDAP injection prevention. If user-supplied input is used to construct LDAP queries without escaping (e.g., skill search queries that get forwarded to LDAP), this is exploitable. Severity: High.

Webhook receiver (Steps 7, 8) — webhook endpoints that accept external payloads require HMAC signature validation. This is called out in Step 8's attack surface list but no concrete implementation requirement is specified in Step 7's API design. Severity: Medium.
SBOM generated once (Step 8) but no continuous dependency monitoring defined. New CVEs in transitive dependencies will go undetected after the initial scan. Severity: Medium.

Search history persisted in localStorage with a 50-entry limit (Step 21) — if queries contain sensitive keywords (e.g., searching for confidential project names), this data is accessible to any script running in the browser context. Severity: Low-Medium depending on data sensitivity.
final_review.summary: This is an ambitious, well-structured plan that covers the right phases in the right order with generally sensible acceptance criteria. The compliance, security threat modeling, and HITL pattern are notably well-considered. However, several concrete failure modes make this plan risky to execute as written. The most critical: collaborative editing is catastrophically underscoped — no WebSocket infrastructure exists anywhere in the plan, yet it appears as a checklist item in a single backend step; this will either silently die or consume 2–3x the estimated effort. The MRR acceptance criterion in Step 15 is unverifiable without a baseline. Elasticsearch's SSPL licensing is an unacknowledged legal and cost risk. JWT in localStorage is a likely implementation path given no explicit guidance, creating a P0 auth vulnerability. SSO/SAML absence will fail enterprise procurement. The dependency graph also has a gap: frontend modules (Steps 19–21) are not wired as prerequisites to system testing (Step 28), allowing tests to pass CI gates before the UI is actually built. Fix the collaborative editing scope (defer or split), mandate httpOnly cookies, add SSO, resolve the Elasticsearch licensing question, and define the WebSocket infrastructure before committing to this plan.

flaws: Step 11 lists 'collaborative editing support via operational transforms or CRDT-based conflict resolution' as one bullet among many wiki features. Real-time collaborative editing (à la Google Docs) is a multi-month engineering effort requiring a WebSocket server, presence protocol, and conflict resolution engine. No WebSocket infrastructure is mentioned anywhere in the plan. This will either be silently dropped or block the entire wiki module.

Step 15 acceptance criterion: 'Re-ranker improves top-10 retrieval MRR by ≥15% on test query set.' There is no golden query set established in any earlier step, and no baseline MRR measured. This criterion is unverifiable as written — you cannot guarantee a relative improvement without a measured baseline.

Step 18 acceptance criterion mentions JWT storage across page reloads. The plan never specifies where JWT tokens are stored. If implemented in localStorage (the default for most React JWT tutorials), this is a P0 XSS vulnerability — any injected script exfiltrates the token. HttpOnly cookies are required for enterprise auth.

Step 23 requires Postgres logical replication (CDC) but wal_level=logical must be set at Postgres initialization time. This is a database configuration concern that belongs in Step 6 (database schema) or Step 24 (infrastructure), not Step 23. If the DB is deployed without it, enabling CDC requires a Postgres restart — production downtime.

Elasticsearch 8.x uses SSPL licensing for production self-hosted deployments in many configurations. Enterprise production use typically requires a commercial Elastic license. The plan has no licensing cost analysis or mitigation (e.g., OpenSearch as a drop-in alternative). This is a legal and budget risk that could force a mid-project replatform.

Step 16 lists 'semantic_drift' as a freshness scoring signal with no specification of how it is computed. Detecting semantic drift requires periodically re-embedding content and comparing cosine distance to a baseline — this is a non-trivial ML pipeline not described anywhere. It will either be silently omitted or require significant unplanned work.

Step 14 (LDAP/SCIM sync) has no conflict resolution strategy defined. In large enterprises, SCIM delta syncs frequently produce duplicate identity records when external IDs change (e.g., contractor rehire, email change). No de-duplication logic or sync idempotency guarantee is specified.

Step 28 lists 'Failover scenario tested' as an acceptance criterion with zero specification of what failover means (DB primary failure? LLM provider outage? Elasticsearch node loss?), what the expected behavior is, or what the RTO target is. This criterion cannot be evaluated.

Steps 19–21 build the frontend but Step 28 (system tests) only depends on Step 22 (admin panel). If any of steps 19–21 are delayed or broken, the E2E tests in Step 28 still pass its dependency check — the dependency graph does not enforce that all frontend modules are complete before system testing.
suggestions: Split Step 11 into two steps: (a) wiki CRUD + versioning + states, and (b) collaborative editing as a separate, explicitly-scoped step with WebSocket infrastructure (socket.io or native WebSocket), a defined CRDTlibrary (e.g., Yjs or Automerge), and its own acceptance criteria. If timeline is constrained, make collaborative editing a v2 feature with a clear deferral decision in the PRD.

Step 6 must specify wal_level=logical in the Postgres initialization config and reserve a replication slot for the CDC pipeline. Document this in the schema migration README so any fresh DB provisioning includes it.

Replace the relative MRR acceptance criterion in Step 15 with an absolute target or define a mandatory baseline measurement task before Step 15 begins. For example: 'BM25-only baseline MRR on 50-query golden set measured and recorded in eval/baseline.json before re-ranker integration.'
Step 18 must explicitly mandate httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies for JWT storage. Add this as a P0 acceptance criterion. Remove any guidance that implies localStorage for tokens.

Add SSO/SAML 2.0 (or OIDC) as an explicit module. Enterprise knowledge management without SSO will fail procurement reviews. This belongs in Step 7 (API design) and Step 10 (auth implementation) at minimum.

Replace Elasticsearch with OpenSearch (Apache 2.0 license) or include an Elastic commercial license line item in the project budget and legal review (Step 3). The current plan has an unacknowledged licensing risk.

Add a data export / portability endpoint (wiki articles as ZIP/Markdown, FAQ as CSV, decision trees as JSON) to the API design in Step 7. Enterprise buyers require this for vendor lock-in compliance — its absence is a common deal-breaker.

Step 15 cross-encoder re-ranking at P95 ≤ 2s under 50 concurrent users requires either a GPU-backed inference service or aggressive result caching. Add an explicit architecture decision: either deploy the cross-encoder behind a dedicated inference endpoint (TorchServe/Triton) or cache re-ranked results by query hash with a short TTL.
Add Steps 19–21 as explicit dependencies of Step 28 to enforce that all frontend modules are complete before system testing runs.
missing: SSO/SAML 2.0 or OIDC integration — not mentioned anywhere. Mandatory for enterprise customer procurement.
WebSocket server design — required by Step 11 (collaborative editing) and Step 21 (streaming search responses). No WebSocket infrastructure, protocol, or connection management is defined.
Content sanitization for the rich text wiki editor — ProseMirror/TipTap output can contain arbitrary HTML if misconfigured. No mention of DOMPurify or server-side HTML sanitization before storage.

LLM API cost model — the plan uses text-embedding-3-small for ingestion, similarity clustering, freshness drift, expert recommendation, and search. At enterprise scale (thousands of documents, frequent re-indexing), this is a significant recurring cost with no budget estimate or cost cap.

Multi-tenancy model — 'enterprise' typically means multiple departments or customer organizations. The plan defines RBAC roles but never defines whether this is a single-tenant or multi-tenant SaaS deployment. If multi-tenant, every data model and query must be scoped by tenant_id from day one.

Email/notification infrastructure — Step 16 sends freshness alerts to content owners; Step 17 routes HITL proposals. No email provider (SES, SendGrid), template system, or notification preferences model is defined.

File storage service — Step 11 references storing media attachments in 'object storage' but no S3/GCS bucket configuration, CDN setup, signed URL generation, or storage lifecycle policy is specified anywhere.
Malware scanning for file uploads — Step 11 validates type and size but does not scan uploaded files for malware. This is a P0 security control for any enterprise platform accepting user uploads.

pgvector backup and recovery strategy — if the embedding store is corrupted or the PVC is lost, re-embedding all content from scratch can take hours and cost hundreds of dollars. No backup schedule or point-in-time recovery for pgvector is defined.

Data migration tooling — Step 1 mentions a 'migration plan drafted' as a deliverable but no migration step exists in the build plan. Enterprises adopting this platform will have existing knowledge bases (Confluence, SharePoint, Notion) that need import tooling.
API versioning strategy — the OpenAPI spec in Step 7 has no versioning scheme (/v1/, header-based, etc.). Breaking changes in a future version will break all integrations.
security_risks: JWT token storage location unspecified — if defaulted to localStorage, all active sessions are exfiltrable via any XSS vector in the rich text wiki editor or search results. Severity: Critical.

ProseMirror/TipTap rich text output stored and re-rendered without explicit server-side sanitization — a malicious contributor can inject stored XSS payloads that execute for all viewers of a wiki article. Severity: Critical.

Prompt injection in AI search — Step 8 identifies this risk and Step 9 says 'Prompt injection guard rules defined in coordinator system prompt.' System prompt instructions are not a reliable defense; they are bypassable. No input-layer sanitization, output validation, or sandboxed LLM execution model is specified. Severity: High.

File upload endpoint (Step 11) validates type and size but has no malware scanning. A contributor can upload a crafted PDF or SVG containing embedded JavaScript or exploit code. In an enterprise platform, this file is then served to potentially thousands of users. Severity: High.

LDAP/SCIM integration (Step 14) — no mention of LDAP injection prevention. If user-supplied input is used to construct LDAP queries without escaping (e.g., skill search queries that get forwarded to LDAP), this is exploitable. Severity: High.

Webhook receiver (Steps 7, 8) — webhook endpoints that accept external payloads require HMAC signature validation. This is called out in Step 8's attack surface list but no concrete implementation requirement is specified in Step 7's API design. Severity: Medium.
SBOM generated once (Step 8) but no continuous dependency monitoring defined. New CVEs in transitive dependencies will go undetected after the initial scan. Severity: Medium.

Search history persisted in localStorage with a 50-entry limit (Step 21) — if queries contain sensitive keywords (e.g., searching for confidential project names), this data is accessible to any script running in the browser context. Severity: Low-Medium depending on data sensitivity.
history.summary: This is an ambitious, well-structured plan that covers the right phases in the right order with generally sensible acceptance criteria. The compliance, security threat modeling, and HITL pattern are notably well-considered. However, several concrete failure modes make this plan risky to execute as written. The most critical: collaborative editing is catastrophically underscoped — no WebSocket infrastructure exists anywhere in the plan, yet it appears as a checklist item in a single backend step; this will either silently die or consume 2–3x the estimated effort. The MRR acceptance criterion in Step 15 is unverifiable without a baseline. Elasticsearch's SSPL licensing is an unacknowledged legal and cost risk. JWT in localStorage is a likely implementation path given no explicit guidance, creating a P0 auth vulnerability. SSO/SAML absence will fail enterprise procurement. The dependency graph also has a gap: frontend modules (Steps 19–21) are not wired as prerequisites to system testing (Step 28), allowing tests to pass CI gates before the UI is actually built. Fix the collaborative editing scope (defer or split), mandate httpOnly cookies, add SSO, resolve the Elasticsearch licensing question, and define the WebSocket infrastructure before committing to this plan.
